title: Core Concepts in Energy
paragraphs: The first concept in energy is thermodynamics. Energy is conserved, but every conversion loses some usefulness to entropy. That is why engineers care so much about efficiency, heat loss, and waste heat recovery. The first law explains what energy is allowed to do; the second law explains what energy is not allowed to do perfectly.

A second concept is energy density. Fuels, batteries, and nuclear materials differ by orders of magnitude in how much energy they store per kilogram or per litre. That single fact shapes transport, storage, military logistics, aviation, and the entire design of the modern energy system.

Capacity factor is another essential idea. A power plant is not useful only because of its peak output. It is useful because of how much energy it delivers over time. Solar and wind have lower capacity factors than coal, gas, or nuclear, which means grids need storage, transmission, flexibility, or backup generation to keep supply reliable.

Energy carriers and primary sources also matter. Sunlight, uranium, coal, natural gas, and flowing water are not interchangeable at the system level. Electricity is a carrier, not a source, and hydrogen is usually a carrier too. The distinction matters because the economics of converting one form into another can dominate the whole project.

Finally, energy systems are never just technical. They are market systems and political systems. Tariffs, subsidies, grid rules, permitting, and capital cost all shape which technologies get built. In practice, the cheapest kilowatt-hour is only the starting point.
keyPoints: Thermodynamics sets the hard limits on energy conversion and efficiency
Energy density explains why some fuels are suitable for planes, cars, grids, and others are not
Capacity factor measures how much useful energy a system actually delivers over time
Electricity is a carrier, not a primary source
Hydrogen is also usually a carrier, not a source
Grid economics and regulation strongly shape real-world energy deployment
Technical merit alone does not determine which energy technologies win
System design matters more than any single component

furtherReading.title: Sustainable Energy - Without the Hot Air
furtherReading.author: David J. C. MacKay
furtherReading.description: The clearest quantitative introduction to energy systems thinking.

furtherReading.title: Power, Speed, and Grace
furtherReading.author: John Doerr
furtherReading.description: A systems-level view of the clean energy transition and the institutions behind it.

furtherReading.title: The Quest
furtherReading.author: Daniel Yergin
furtherReading.description: A broad history of how energy technologies and geopolitics evolved together.
